title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a market organiser called Lucy talking about a work-experience scheme at a local food market. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a market organiser called Lucy talking about a work-experience scheme at a local food market. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: A market organiser called Lucy introduces a work-experience scheme.
extracts.audio_script: Good morning. I’m Lucy, and I organise Fresh Square Market, which sells local food every Saturday. We are offering a new work-experience scheme for young people interested in retail, food or starting a small business. The market does not take place in the modern shopping street. It is held inside a restored [9]bus station[/9], where vehicles stopped operating fifteen years ago. Its wide covered area gives stalls protection from rain while remaining open and lively for visitors. The old ticket office is now our small information point.||Around thirty producers attend regularly, selling vegetables, bread, cheese and prepared food. Students joining the scheme will meet several of them. Visitors often ask which item sells in the greatest quantity. Although fresh bread is popular early in the morning, the product purchased most often over the full day is [10]local honey[/10], sold by two beekeepers from villages nearby. Before students begin practical work, everyone attends a session on [11]customer communication[/11], because answering questions politely and listening carefully are essential skills at any stall.||Students rotate between duties. They may help prepare signs, guide shoppers or collect feedback. Their first task on a market morning is usually [12]setting up displays[/12], arranging goods neatly under the stallholder's direction before customers arrive. Participants should wear comfortable shoes and clothing suitable for changing weather. We provide name badges and reusable aprons. Students are asked to bring a [13]clipboard[/13], because they record stock information and visitor comments during their shifts. Mobile phones are not practical when hands are busy moving products or handing out leaflets.||We try to make the market environmentally responsible. Stallholders are encouraged to avoid unnecessary packaging, and visitors can return clean containers. This spring we have introduced a [14]refill station[/14], where people can purchase certain dry foods using their own jars or bags. Students will help explain how it works. We also want young participants to learn from producers. The additional talk with the highest number of student registrations is on [15]bread making[/15], led by a baker who began a small business from home and now sells every Saturday.||Students who complete four Saturdays will receive a [16]market reference[/16] describing their responsibilities, which they may include with future applications. At the end of summer, the producers and students will join our first [17]harvest supper[/17], an informal outdoor meal using ingredients sold by the market. Tickets will raise money for our improvement plan. At present, the covered space is accessible, but the entrance surface becomes difficult when wet. Funds from the supper will be used to create a safer [18]stone pathway[/18] from the car park to the market. Application forms for students can be collected from the information point today.

dialogue.speaker: lucy

questions.explanation: Lucy says the market takes place in a restored 'bus station'.

questions.explanation: Lucy identifies 'local honey' as the most frequently purchased product.

questions.explanation: Lucy says students first learn about 'customer communication'.
